findings.title: Rewindable check can fire even when body is None (permanent_redirect path)
findings.detail: For a 308 Permanent Redirect, `body` is kept (not set to None) and headers are retained. But `_body_position` is set at prepare-time even for bodies that are not file-like streams (it is None for non-streams, so that's fine). However, if `body` has been reset to None by a previous redirect hop and a *second* redirect occurs, `_body_position` is still the original int from prep-time. The check `prepared_request._body_position is not None` would still be True, yet `prepared_request.body` is None. `rewind_body` would then call `getattr(None, 'seek', None)` → None, and fall into the `else` branch raising `UnrewindableBodyError`. This can occur in a redirect chain mixing non-307/308 and 307/308 hops. Guarding on `prepared_request.body is not None` in addition to the current checks would prevent this.

findings.title: Rewind check uses post-purge headers, missing body=None case
findings.detail: The `rewindable` check reads `headers` (line 152) which is `prepared_request.headers`. When the status code is NOT a 307/308, the block at lines 145–150 sets `prepared_request.body = None` AND removes Content-Length / Transfer-Encoding. So `rewindable` will be False for those codes because the headers have been stripped — which is actually correct for 301/302/303 redirects (body is intentionally dropped). However, the logic is fragile and non-obvious: it relies on the side-effect of header removal rather than an explicit check on the status code or body. A clearer guard such as `if prepared_request.body is not None` (i.e. body wasn't cleared above) would be more readable and robust, and is less likely to accidentally regress if the header-purging logic changes in the future.

findings.title: Typo in error message: 'occured' should be 'occurred'
findings.detail: Line 289: `"An error occured when rewinding request "` — 'occured' is misspelled and should be 'occurred'. This also mismatches the test at line 1482 which asserts `'error occured when rewinding request body' in str(e)`, so fixing one requires fixing both.

findings.title: test_rewind_body_failed_seek asserts wrong substring
findings.detail: Line 1482: `assert 'error occured when rewinding request body' in str(e)` — in pytest, `str(e)` on a `pytest.ExceptionInfo` object includes the repr of the exception including its type name. The assertion relies on matching a lowercase substring of the message; this is fine in practice but note that the `e` here is the `ExceptionInfo` wrapper, not the exception itself. The test would be clearer (and more precise) using `str(e.value)` to match against the exception message directly.

findings.title: tell() is called before body is consumed, recording start position is correct but the comment is misleading
findings.detail: The comment says 'Record the current file position before reading', but the body is not read here — it is read later by urllib3 when the request is actually sent. The comment should say 'Record the current file position so we can rewind before any redirect resend' for accuracy.

findings.title: No test for redirect chain where rewind fails mid-chain
findings.detail: The tests cover `rewind_body` in isolation and a single 307 redirect with a seekable body, but there is no test verifying the behaviour when `rewind_body` raises `UnrewindableBodyError` during `resolve_redirects` — i.e. that the exception propagates correctly to the caller and doesn't silently swallow the error or leave the session in a broken state.

findings.title: Sentinel object() for failed tell() does not survive copy()
findings.detail: When `body.tell()` raises an exception, `self._body_position` is set to a freshly created `object()` instance (line 464). In `copy()` (line 326), `p._body_position = self._body_position` copies the reference — this is fine. However the intent is to use `isinstance(prepared_request._body_position, int)` as the gate in `rewind_body`. The sentinel works for distinguishing from `None` and from a valid `int`, but its type (`object`) is not documented or checked anywhere explicitly. The approach is correct but fragile; using a dedicated sentinel constant (e.g., `_UNSET = object()` at module level) or a named namedtuple/enum value would make the intent clearer and prevent accidental creation of multiple distinct sentinels.

findings.title: PEP-8: comparison to None should use `is None`
findings.detail: Line 1408: `assert prep._body_position == None` should be `assert prep._body_position is None`. The rest of the tests (e.g. line 1498) correctly use `is not None`.

findings.title: Docstring grammar: 'it's' should be 'its'
findings.detail: Line 282: `"Move file pointer back to it's recorded starting position"` — 'it\'s' (it is) should be 'its' (possessive).

findings.title: Missing blank line between RetryError and UnrewindableBodyError
findings.detail: The existing code uses a blank line between each exception class definition (see lines 100–102). `UnrewindableBodyError` at line 103 is missing the preceding blank line that follows the pattern. PEP-8 recommends two blank lines around top-level definitions.
